gist: Jordi Visser argues Bitcoin's 50% drawdown is a 'rotation' within a bear-market regime (price below 200-day MA), not a structural collapse, and that the bigger 2H story is a rotation out of AI hardware/chips into the application layer — specifically 'human software' (Eli Lilly/peptides) and 'financial guardrails' (crypto/tokenization). He frames Bitcoin as a long-duration durability holding and a direct play on AI agents overtaking humans in commerce, while flagging real bottleneck/revenue risk for the generalist AI model layer.

themes.theme: Bitcoin regime: bear-market technicals & the rotation thesis

insights.claim: Bitcoin is down ~50% from its all-time high and remains in a bear-market regime because it failed at the 200-day moving average a few weeks ago and turned lower, so Jordi Visser will not buy aggressively until it bases and reclaims a long-term moving average.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Treat the 200-day (and 200-week) MA reclaim as the buy trigger; until then, expect continued slow bleed rather than a bottom.
insights.quote: we failed at the 200 day moving average a few weeks ago. We started to go down again all while the stock market was going up nine weeks in a row.

insights.claim: Bitcoin has decoupled from the stock market — it fell ~50% while the S&P rose nine weeks in a row — which Visser views as a necessary precondition for crypto's eventual wealth transfer out of the fiat system, even though near-term it removes equity-beta support.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Don't expect equity strength to rescue BTC near term; the decoupling is structurally bullish for Visser's long thesis but bearish for short-term tape support.
insights.quote: the good news is Bitcoin's not correlated to the stock market anymore. The bad news is uh it's down 50% off the highs.

insights.claim: Visser sees the market entering a 3-to-6 month 'rotation' where capital moves away from the AI-hardware winners (chips/memory/infrastructure) toward laggard groups, and he is positioning for software — but not traditional SaaS — to catch a bid.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Rotate exposure out of crowded chip/memory leaders into the application layer over the next 1-2 quarters.
insights.quote: I believe we're at a rotation point that's going to last for at least the next 3 to 6 months.

insights.claim: Bitcoin has been 'lumped in' with AI-adjacent equities (crypto miners, cyber, AI-software names) rather than trading as pure software, which Visser cites as a reason its selloff tracked the broader risk complex.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Watch whether BTC starts trading on its own narrative vs. as an AI-risk proxy; the mischaracterization may unwind as rotation plays out.

insights.claim: Visser dismisses the four-year halving cycle as having no predictive value, noting this is the first 'four-year cycle' where stocks rose while Bitcoin fell, and reframes the relevant long-term level as the 200-week (~4-year) moving average instead.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Stop anchoring entries to the halving calendar; use the 200-week MA as the durable trend reference instead.
insights.quote: the 200WE moving average is a really important thing... that's kind of a 4-year moving average. So, if the four-year cycle is this, it's fine.

insights.claim: Visser explicitly flags the 60,000 level as a downside zone he is willing to let Bitcoin break, saying it could trade below that without changing his long-term stance.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: 60k is not a defended level in his framework; don't assume it holds as support.
insights.quote: Let it break through the 60,000 level. Maybe it goes down even below that. I don't really care.

insights.claim: Visser argues prior textbook 'sell-the-news' events — the spot-ETF launch and the US government endorsing crypto plus a presidential memecoin in 2024-25 — were instead bought, and that the real distribution has been OG holders churning out since 2024.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The overhang is long-time-holder distribution, not a single catalyst; wait for BTC to rise on bad news as the all-clear signal.
insights.quote: I do think there's been churn that's been happening now since 2024... until the charts start giving you hope and you get a it going higher on bad news type thing, I'm not going to get too into it.

insights.claim: Visser says he has never sold a single Bitcoin and won't, treating it as a multi-decade durability holding, but he actively trades MicroStrategy in and out repeatedly rather than holding it.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Distinguish core BTC (hold) from the levered equity proxy (trade); MSTR is his tactical vehicle, not a buy-and-hold.
insights.quote: I've never sold a single one in it, single Bitcoin. I have traded Micro Strategy many many times. Got in, got out, got in, got out.

insights.claim: Visser is accumulating Ethereum throughout the drawdown as a bet on crypto network effects being the dominant story over the next 12 months, treating ETH as a proxy for the broader ecosystem rather than a standalone Bitcoin substitute.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: He is adding ETH on weakness as an ecosystem/network-effect play — a notable tilt for a Bitcoin-centric audience.
insights.quote: I've been buying Ethereum during this whole drop. So, I'm happy it's going down because I'm putting bits and pieces in.

insights.claim: Visser recommends a 2-3% Bitcoin allocation as appropriate for essentially any investor regardless of belief, calling both a 100% BTC concentration and a sub-1% allocation 'very stupid'.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A concrete sizing rule: 2-3% BTC as a portfolio resilience sleeve, not a max-conviction bet.
insights.quote: I believe that it should be at this point in everyone's portfolio at least 2 to 3% regardless of what they believe in.

insights.claim: Visser built an equal-weighted index of 40 crypto-ecosystem names (34 tokens, 6 public stocks) that is highly correlated to Bitcoin even when Bitcoin is excluded, which he reads as confirmation that Bitcoin is 'the S&P 500 of crypto' and the ecosystem is behaving as expected.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Crypto remains a single high-beta factor; diversifying across tokens does not meaningfully reduce BTC correlation.
insights.quote: when I create an equal weight of 40 separate companies, 34 of them being cryptocurrencies or tokens... magically it's extremely correlated to Bitcoin, even when you don't include Bitcoin in it.

insights.claim: Visser frames Bitcoin as a multi-generational 'durability' asset using the Patek Philippe analogy and holds a strategic Bitcoin reserve for his children, prioritizing 50-60 year resilience over short-term return.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: His BTC thesis is duration/inheritance-driven, not cycle-timing; explains why drawdowns don't shake him out.
insights.quote: I don't know of an asset more than Bitcoin that people have confidence in over some very long period of time, 50 years, 60 years, is going to continue to do that.

insights.claim: Visser acknowledges Bitcoin going to zero is a non-trivial part of the outcome distribution, but assigns quantum-computing destroying it a very low probability, and holds BTC small enough that a total loss would not be life-changing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Even the bull sizes BTC as a tail-aware bet; quantum is a known but low-probability kill risk on his radar.
insights.quote: Do I think Bitcoin might go to zero? Sure. It's part of the distribution of outcomes.

themes.theme: Volatility regime: BTC vs. single-stock dispersion

insights.claim: Bitcoin's 50% crash has occurred with collapsing volatility — bleeding ~2% per week rather than 15% single-day drops — which Visser argues makes BTC newly acceptable as an institutional asset by removing its 'too volatile' objection.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Falling BTC realized vol is a structural positive for institutional adoption even amid the price decline.
insights.quote: this 50% crash has occurred with volatility collapsing... Bitcoin's bleeding like 2% a week.

insights.claim: Leading individual stocks are moving 8-10% per day and single-stock-to-index volatility ratios are at the highest ever recorded, which Visser attributes to retail/zero-DTE option flow and reads as the market training itself for parabolas and speed-crashes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Equity single-name vol now exceeds BTC's; expect more parabolic moves and fast crashes driven by 0DTE/retail flow.
insights.quote: every day we're watching the leading stocks in the stock market move 8 to 10% a day... the highest volatility relative to index vault that we've ever seen.

insights.claim: Visser argues AI is compressing market time so that former four-year crypto cycles may now run as ~9-month cycles, meaning bull and bear markets occur within the same parabolic-and-crash price action.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Shorten cycle-timing assumptions; volatility and round-trips will happen far faster than historical cadence implies.
insights.quote: maybe these aren't four-year cycles maybe these are now 9mon cycles and we've compress the time and that's what a bubble is. It's price versus time.

themes.theme: AI capex, bottlenecks & the model-layer revenue problem

insights.claim: Visser explicitly says this is NOT a stock-market bubble but a sign that AI requires enormous capital now, citing the wave of IPOs, Google's $85B raise, and Berkshire taking a $10B stake in a company up 120% YoY as signals the market is underestimating.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Read the IPO/large-raise wave as capital formation for AI buildout, not late-cycle froth; Berkshire's participation is a tell.
insights.quote: I think these are a sign of something important. It's not a stock market bubble to me, but it is a sign that people need a lot of capital right now for what is happening with inside the AI world.

insights.claim: The cost of a 1-gigawatt data center has inflated from ~$50B to $60-80B in roughly nine months, with Jensen Huang guiding to $80-100B soon, illustrating severe AI infrastructure bottleneck inflation.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Datacenter capex per gigawatt is inflating ~60-100% in under a year — a margin and financing risk for the buildout.
insights.quote: a data center for 1 gawatt has gone from 50 billion he was saying it's 60 to 80 and Jensen Yuang said in Computex this weekend it'll very quickly be 80 to 100 billion.

insights.claim: Of Jensen Huang's five-layer AI stack (chips, energy, infrastructure, models, applications), only chips/energy/infrastructure are currently monetized, and Visser warns that at projected per-gigawatt costs the revenues may not arrive fast enough to justify the spend.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: The model and application layers are the unmonetized weak link; revenue timing is the key risk to the whole capex cycle.
insights.quote: The only thing that's been monetized or the only thing making money are the chips, the energy, and the infrastructure. There's a problem with that eventually.

insights.claim: Visser argues generalist/foundation models face structural margin pressure: DeepSeek competes directly at the model layer, enterprise adoption of generalist models may not materialize, and he doubts a generalist model can sustainably make money.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Be skeptical of generalist-model economics; the edge accrues to specialized/proprietary-data models.
insights.quote: I'm not sure a generalist model anymore you're going to be able to make the money. I don't think the adoption is going to happen on the enterprise side.

insights.claim: Visser cautions that Anthropic's quoted ~$44B figure is ARR (annualized run-rate), not realized revenue, and that parabolic AI growth can break quickly if it tweaks even for a month — citing OpenAI's past year as the cautionary example.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Discount headline AI ARR numbers; parabolic run-rates are fragile and a single soft month can repriced the whole narrative.
insights.quote: when we talk about enthropic and we're like it's 44 it's not 44 billion that's the ARR... going like this for 3 months does not ensure you'll be doing that for another 3 years.

insights.claim: Enterprises (Amazon, JP Morgan, Uber) are reportedly blowing through full-year AI token budgets in a single quarter and are now actively engineering token efficiency, which compresses per-customer/per-token revenue for model providers even as aggregate revenue grows.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Per-token monetization is eroding as customers optimize; aggregate provider growth masks deteriorating unit economics.
insights.quote: They're all reporting that they blew through their token budgets, you know, in a single quarter for the entire year.

insights.claim: Visser cites the 'edge' (on-device/edge inference) as the structural bearish argument for all model providers, arguing edge capability is arriving sooner than expected and undermining the centralized data-center borrowing-and-buildout model.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Edge inference is a medium-term threat to centralized model-provider economics; watch the timeline compress.
insights.quote: the bearish argument for all of the model providers is eventually going to be the edge. So the problem with the edge is the edge is coming sooner than what people think.

themes.theme: LLM commoditization & specialized vs. generalist models

insights.claim: Visser argues LLMs are now commoditized: he finds Claude and ChatGPT 'indistinguishable' in capability and has migrated his primary use to GPT-5.5 (via Codex/Cursor) because it is a better brainstorming partner that anticipates his intent, not because of raw model superiority.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Differentiation among frontier LLMs is shifting from capability to fit/UX; pricing power at the model layer is weakening.
insights.quote: the LLMs are now commoditized... Claude and Chat GPT they're indistinguishable to me in terms of now being able to use them.

insights.claim: Generalist model providers are effectively conceding the specialized-workflow market by spinning up internal verticals and JVs (biotech teams, PE accounting rollups), an admission that their generalist models can't win narrow domains like finance and healthcare.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Vertical/specialized AI with proprietary data is the durable moat; generalist providers are pivoting to capture it via JVs.
insights.quote: they're admitting it because what are they doing? They're creating JVS. They're creating internal teams.

insights.claim: Enterprises are testing replacement of paid frontier models with fine-tuned open-source models (e.g. one firm moving from Claude to DeepSeek V4) to cut costs while claiming ~90%+ performance, a dynamic to watch over the next 30-60 days.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Open-source/fine-tuned substitution is an emerging cost-driven threat to paid-model revenue; near-term inflection to monitor.
insights.quote: they have completely uh eradicated the use of cloud internally and they've gone to Deep Seek V4.

insights.claim: Both speakers name XAI as underrated for its ability to come from nowhere and gain adoption, alongside continued strength for Anthropic and OpenAI, framing AI as a pie-expanding rather than zero-sum market.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Don't treat the frontier-model race as zero-sum; xAI is a sleeper to watch.

themes.theme: Eli Lilly, peptides & 'human software' as the next leg

insights.claim: Visser predicts Eli Lilly has a chance to become the largest company in the world and the #1 AI company within 5 years because it is building a specialized model on 150 years of proprietary metabolic-disease data plus a 1,000-GPU/1,000-Blackwell in-house data center.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Highest-conviction single-name call in the episode: Eli Lilly as the prototypical proprietary-data 'human software' AI winner.
insights.quote: Eli Liy... has a chance to be the largest company in the world and the number one AI company in the world within 5 years is because they're building a specialized model.

insights.claim: Eli Lilly's advantage is that it doesn't depend on third-party tokens — it monetizes directly by selling better drugs and making FDA approval more efficient — which Visser contrasts favorably against unmonetized generalist AI bets.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Favor AI plays with direct revenue and owned compute over those dependent on external token economics.
insights.quote: if a drug company can make their entire process of getting the FDA approval efficient and at the same time they can benefit by selling better drugs well they're getting revenue directly.

insights.claim: Eli Lilly recently stopped disclosing its phase-one trials — a defensive shift Visser reads as protecting IP from AI-accelerated competitive catch-up rather than serving investors.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A signal that pharma IP defense is now an AI-competition issue; reduced disclosure is a deliberate moat-protection move.
insights.quote: Eli Lilly stopped disclosing phase one trials they're working on... because it's much easier to catch up to IP if you have AI.

insights.claim: Visser identifies the obesity TAM at $200-300B, largely untapped due to cost and lack of insurance coverage, with Eli Lilly working on single annual treatments and pill (not shot) formats to expand access.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Massive under-penetrated TAM; cheaper/less-frequent dosing is the unlock that drives the next growth leg.
insights.quote: The TAM market for obesity in the in the world is between 200 and $300 billion... very little of it right now is being accessed because of the cost.

insights.claim: Visser frames peptides as the 'API key for the human body' — receptors targeted to make the body act differently — and argues GLP-1 'side effects' (reduced addiction, etc.) are actually new monetizable indications Eli Lilly can investigate.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Each peptide is multi-indication optionality; 'side effects' are pipeline expansion, widening the TAM well beyond weight loss.
insights.quote: peptides are the API key for the human body

insights.claim: GLP-1 drugs reducing addiction/risk-taking is cited via the anecdote that some hedge funds barred employees from taking GLP-1s because it dampened risk-taking, pointing to a large potential drug/alcohol-addiction treatment market.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Addiction treatment is a large second-order GLP-1 market; watch for recovery-center/rehab commercialization.
insights.quote: some of the hedge funds on Wall Street were requiring people to not take GOP1 because it took away some of the risk-taking genes

insights.claim: Eli Lilly is making strategic acquisitions almost weekly (sometimes multiple per week) tied to diabetes-adjacent areas where GLP-1s showed impact (kidneys, cancers), mirroring how the Mag-7 bought Instagram-style 'API key' assets.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Lilly is rolling up the peptide/metabolic stack; M&A cadence is a leading indicator of its platform ambitions.
insights.quote: they are buying companies almost every single week and in some weeks they're buying multiple companies

insights.claim: Visser cites Victoria's Secret's stock up 47% on 15% sales growth attributed in large part to GLP-1-driven weight loss as evidence of unexpected positive second-order effects across the consumer economy.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: GLP-1 ripple effects create both losers (food) and winners (apparel); screen for non-obvious beneficiaries.
insights.quote: Victoria Secret put their earnings out there... The stock was up 47% earlier this week. They reported 15% sales growth... directly attributable or in a large part to GLP1's people are losing weight.

insights.claim: Visser highlights that healthcare is approaching ~20% of US annual personal expenditures and growing on demographics, framing it as the largest market to focus attention on and the basis for entitlement-reform optionality if peptides/gene-editing let people 'die healthy instead of die sick'.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Healthcare/human-software is the dominant secular TAM; longevity tech also reframes the entitlement/debt outlook.
insights.quote: we're we're almost at 20% of uh personal expenditures in the country each year are healthcare.

themes.theme: Macro: rates, liquidity, debt & geopolitical bottlenecks

insights.claim: Visser anchors his next-up-leg-in-Bitcoin thesis on three-month rates currently sitting below inflation, framing negative real short rates as the macro fuel for the next durable BTC trend.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Negative real short rates are his core macro tailwind; the next BTC trend higher is the one he considers important.
insights.quote: we right now have three-month rates that are below inflation. Just wait for the next trend higher in Bitcoin.

insights.claim: Visser is not worried about US sovereign debt because household net worth is roughly five times the size of the national debt, but flags entitlements (Medicare/Medicaid driven by the country's health) as the genuine fiscal problem.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes the debt-doom narrative: the binding constraint is entitlement/health spending, not the headline debt level.
insights.quote: I've never worried about the debt of the country... because the household net worth of the country is five times the size of the debt of the country.

insights.claim: Visser flags that the Strait of Hormuz remains shut and that prediction markets price a full reopening to normal before the midterms at under 50%, urging investors to weight prediction-market odds over confident reopening narratives.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A live energy/supply bottleneck with sub-50% near-term resolution odds — a macro risk that compounds AI buildout shortages.
insights.quote: the straight of Hormuz is still shut... right now for the straight to completely reopen to normal by right before the midterms is less than 50%.

insights.claim: Visser argues the market needs 'more bubbles and more billionaires' because bubbles are the mechanism that pulls in the capital required to fund AI infrastructure, reframing bubble dynamics as necessary capital formation.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive macro frame: bubble-like capital raising is a feature funding the buildout, not a bearish signal to fade.
insights.quote: we need more bubbles and we need more billionaires

themes.theme: Bitcoin & crypto as the financial layer of the AI-agent economy

insights.claim: Visser's headline thesis is that Bitcoin is a direct play on AI agents overtaking humans in commerce, citing that there are already more AI agents than humans on HTML sites, so the future of commerce — and the asset that captures it — is agent-driven.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes BTC's bull case around agentic commerce rather than store-of-value; the agent-economy adoption curve is the thing to track.
insights.quote: I believe Bitcoin is a direct play on agents dominating humans going forward.

insights.claim: Visser argues the two software categories that matter going forward are 'human software' (the body, via peptides/gene-editing) and 'financial guardrails' (crypto/tokenization), because AI agents will operate in both — and these, not traditional SaaS, are where he is focused.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: His unifying thesis: position in human-software (Eli Lilly) and financial-guardrail (crypto/tokenization) software, not legacy SaaS.
insights.quote: the software that people are choosing is for the human body and for the financial guardrails because AI agents are both that part of software.

insights.claim: Visser argues no idea or innovation in history lasts because something better always replaces it, and since stocks and tokens are ideas, AI generating ideas faster means individual names/tokens will turn over more rapidly — reinforcing Bitcoin as the rare durable constant.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Faster innovation turnover argues for owning the durable index-like asset (BTC) over rotating individual names/tokens.
insights.quote: I don't believe any idea or innovation in the history of mankind has ever lasted... if AI is now going to be creating all of the ideas, that means they're not going to be around that long.

insights.claim: Visser does not believe the S&P 500 is safe past 2030 because AI will disrupt all companies — explicitly including Apple, Google, and Amazon — which underpins his preference for Bitcoin's durability over individual mega-cap equities.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A provocative long-horizon call to question passive S&P safety and mega-cap durability beyond 2030.
insights.quote: I don't think investing in the S&P 500 is safe past 2030. I've said that repeatedly because I think AI will disrupt all companies.

insights.claim: Visser applies a mobile-app user-churn framework to both Bitcoin and AI businesses: each run-up converts a net-new cohort of permanent holders/users even as some churn out, so floors ratchet higher over cycles and capital is durably driven into these assets.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Use the net-user-accretion lens: volatility sheds weak hands but each cycle sets a higher floor — a reason to buy drawdowns.
insights.quote: you get these big run-ups in price, a bunch of people pile in, you get it kind of bleeds out, some people leave, but you've converted new people. AI is starting to do this now.
